explicit_text: I lived in Chicago before I was drafted into the Army in December 1942. I worked there as a spot welder for the Industrial Land Company, making disk lamps and stove lamps. Chicago was home to me before the war changed everything.

explicit_text: After being inducted in December 1942, I was sent to Camp Grant where we were examined and given IQ tests. It was one of the first places I trained before being assigned to Camp Claiborne in Louisiana. Camp Grant was the starting point of my military journey.

explicit_text: I was assigned to Camp Claiborne, Louisiana, for basic training and maneuvers in the swamps. It was tough training in harsh conditions, but it prepared us for what was to come overseas. Camp Claiborne was where I learned the basics of soldiering.

explicit_text: After training in Louisiana, I was shipped to Fort Howze in Texas for further preparation before heading overseas. Fort Howze was the last stop in the States before we boarded ships to England in December 1943. It was a crucial staging area for us soldiers.

explicit_text: We landed in England around December 15th or 16th, 1943, and took a train to southern England where we camped in tents for a few days. England was our base before the invasion of France. It was cold and open fields, but it was where we gathered strength for the next phase.

explicit_text: We landed on the beach in France on the 20th during a severe thunderstorm with rough waters. The terrain was muddy and treacherous, and the Germans shelled us heavily as we advanced. We waited for Patton's tanks to organize before pushing forward and eventually moved through France toward Paris and beyond.

explicit_text: General George S. Patton played a key role in our advance after we landed in France. We waited for him to bring his tanks before pushing the Germans back. explicit_text: We stopped outside Paris because the French wanted to take their own city to avoid destruction. After they secured Paris, we turned our attention toward Belgium and Luxembourg. Paris was a significant moment in our campaign as the French reclaimed their capital.

explicit_text: After being wounded and mentally affected, I was sent to a hospital in Tennessee for treatment. It was the closest mental hospital to my home at the time. Tennessee was part of my recovery before being transferred to Fort Custer in Michigan.

explicit_text: Before the war, I worked for the Industrial Land Company in Chicago as a spot welder. We made disk lamps and stove lamps, and later cat-eyes for tanks and jeeps for night driving. The company moved to Indiana after the war, but I chose not to follow.

explicit_text: After the war, I worked at Dryden Rubber Company as a stock boy and cleanup boy because my disability limited my job options. It was hard work, but I needed to support myself. I eventually had a disagreement with my supervisor and left the job.

explicit_text: Before the war, I joined the Civilian Conservation Corps when I was about sixteen or seventeen in Wisconsin. I worked planting evergreens and received $21 a month with clothing and food provided. It was a better deal than my previous farm job and gave me some early work experience.
